agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO described it at a high level during an all-hands meeting, leaving most implementation details, thresholds, and guardrail specifics undefined.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity — the primary input signal for price adjustments"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds that constrain how far automated pricing can deviate from a baseline, preventing extreme prices"},{"term":"Base Price","definition":"Implied but unstated — the reference price from which dynamic adjustments are calculated"},{"term":"Price Adjustment","definition":"The delta applied to a delivery price in response to a demand signal, which can be positive (surge) or negative (discount)"},{"term":"Real-Time","definition":"Pricing changes are computed and applied continuously or near-continuously, not in batch or manual cycles"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline or capacity threshold","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy — the correlation between high demand and higher prices is a strategic choice, not a data integrity constraint. Leadership could decide to cap surges or flatten pricing.","testable_assertion":"Given demand above the high-demand threshold, the computed price must be greater than the base price"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline or capacity threshold","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Lowering prices during low demand is a competitive strategy, not a system integrity constraint. A VP could disable discounting.","testable_assertion":"Given demand below the low-demand threshold, the computed price must be less than the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual approval step in the real-time pricing path","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation is a product design choice. The business could introduce manual override or approval gates without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a demand signal change, a new price must be computed and applied without human intervention"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Guardrails must exist that constrain the price within an upper bound (price ceiling) and a lower bound (price floor)","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without bounds, an automated system could produce prices of $0 or $999,999 — violating financial integrity, potentially regulatory requirements, and customer trust. A price must always be a positive, bounded value. The specific thresholds are policy, but the existence of bounds is an invariant.","testable_assertion":"For any computed price P: price_floor <= P <= price_ceiling, where price_floor > 0"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The price floor must be greater than zero — a delivery can never be free or negative-priced","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A zero or negative delivery price would mean the company pays the customer to deliver, which violates basic financial integrity. No business policy change makes a negative price valid.","testable_assertion":"For any computed price P: P > 0"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Price adjustments must be driven by demand signals, not arbitrary or random factors","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The demand-driven model is a business strategy. The company could later add weather, time-of-day, or other signals. The constraint is policy, not integrity.","testable_assertion":"Every price change event must reference a demand measurement as its causal input"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The system must balance competitiveness and profitability — prices should not be so high they destroy demand, nor so low they are unprofitable","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a strategic objective, not a hard constraint. The thresholds defining 'competitive' and 'profitable' are business policy decisions.","testable_assertion":"The price floor must be >= the cost of fulfillment (margin >= 0), and the price ceiling must be validated against market rate benchmarks"}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., flash event, natural disaster)","expected_behavior":"Guardrails must cap the price at the ceiling regardless of demand magnitude. The system must not produce unbounded prices."},{"scenario":"Demand drops to zero","expected_behavior":"Price must not go below the floor. The floor must still cover minimum operational cost."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly (flapping)","expected_behavior":"Unclear from spec — system may need smoothing/dampening to avoid price whiplash that confuses customers. UNDEFINED."},{"scenario":"Guardrail values are misconfigured (floor > ceiling)","expected_behavior":"System must reject invalid guardrail configuration and refuse to operate with inconsistent bounds."},{"scenario":"Multiple simultaneous demand signals disagree","expected_behavior":"Undefined — no aggregation strategy specified. System needs a canonical demand measurement."},{"scenario":"Price is computed but delivery is already quoted to customer","expected_behavior":"Undefined — no mention of price lock / quote validity window. Critical gap."},{"scenario":"Regulatory price-gouging laws apply in the delivery region","expected_behavior":"Undefined — no mention of regulatory compliance. Guardrails may need to be jurisdiction-aware."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue per delivery and total demand volume. Correct implementation could optimize margin; incorrect implementation could either leave money on the table (floor too low, ceiling too low) or destroy demand (prices too aggressive). The absence of defined thresholds is a financial risk.","operational":"Full automation reduces manual pricing overhead but introduces operational risk — a bug or bad demand signal could reprice the entire catalog instantly. Monitoring, alerting, and circuit-breaker mechanisms are implied but not specified.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price fairness perception is a major UX concern. No mention of how price changes are communicated to users.","risk":"HIGH. The spec is extremely vague. An automated system that adjusts prices in real time with undefined guardrails, no regulatory consideration, and no price-lock semantics is a significant business and legal risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Will pay variable prices — trust, fairness perception, and price transparency are critical concerns"},{"group":"Finance/Revenue","impact":"Directly affected — dynamic pricing changes margin per delivery and total revenue"},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Demand shifts caused by pricing affect courier allocation and capacity planning"},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Demand fluctuation affects earnings and route density — unclear if courier pay is also dynamic"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Price-gouging regulations vary by jurisdiction — no mention of compliance guardrails"},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Variable pricing will generate support tickets ('why was my price different?') — no explanation framework defined"},{"group":"Product/Marketing","impact":"Must communicate pricing model to users — no transparency requirements specified"}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is a 60-second all-hands soundbite, not a product spec. Almost every critical detail is undefined: demand thresholds, price floors/ceilings, adjustment algorithms, price-lock windows, regulatory compliance, courier pay implications, customer communication, and monitoring strategy. The only things stated with confidence are the general direction (demand-up = price-up, demand-down = price-down) and the desire for automation with guardrails. Everything else is inference.","high_confidence_areas":["Prices go up when demand is high","Prices go down when demand is low","System is automated","Some form of guardrails will exist"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific guardrail thresholds (floor/ceiling values)","Definition and measurement of 'demand'","Price-lock or quote validity semantics","Regulatory compliance requirements","How 'competitive' and 'profitable' are quantified","Whether courier compensation is also dynamic","Smoothing/dampening behavior for rapid demand changes","Customer-facing transparency requirements"],"assumptions":["A base price exists from which adjustments are calculated","Price floor must be positive (no free or negative-priced deliveries)","Guardrails include both upper and lower bounds","'Real-time' means near-continuous, not daily batch","Demand is measurable via some existing signal (order volume, request rate, etc.)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The CEO said 'probably have some guardrails so prices don't go crazy' — it is unclear whether guardrails are confirmed requirements or aspirational. The word 'probably' introduces ambiguity about whether bounds are mandatory.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Finance","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["Treat guardrails as mandatory (invariant)","Treat guardrails as optional/phase-2"],"recommendation":"Treat guardrails as mandatory. An unbounded automated pricing system is an existential business risk. Escalate to CEO for confirmation."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No definition of 'demand' — is it order volume per hour, requests per zone, courier utilization ratio, or something else? The entire system hinges on this signal and it is undefined.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Operations"],"options":["Define demand as order volume per time window per zone","Define demand as courier utilization ratio","Composite signal"],"recommendation":"Escalate to Product for a precise demand metric definition before any implementation begins."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of price-lock semantics. If a customer sees a price, starts checkout, and demand spikes mid-transaction, does the price change? This is a critical UX and legal concern.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Legal/Compliance","Customer Support"],"options":["Lock price at quote time for N minutes","Allow price to change until payment confirmation","Lock at cart, re-validate at checkout"],"recommendation":"Escalate to Product and Legal. Price-lock policy must be defined before launch."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of regulatory compliance. Many jurisdictions have price-gouging laws, especially during emergencies. Dynamic pricing during a natural disaster could be illegal.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Customers","Executive"],"options":["Jurisdiction-aware ceiling overrides","Global emergency pricing freeze capability","Ignore regulatory risk"],"recommendation":"Escalate to Legal immediately. This is a non-negotiable compliance requirement that must inform guardrail design."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No specification of guardrail values, who sets them, or how they are updated. Are they static config, admin-editable, or algorithmically derived?","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Finance","Operations"],"options":["Static configuration","Admin UI for business users","Algorithm-derived with human approval"],"recommendation":"Escalate to Product. Guardrail governance model must be defined."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Specification is insufficient to define acceptance criteria","business_rule_broken":"Cannot verify BR-1 through BR-7 without defined demand metrics, threshold values, guardrail bounds, and price-lock semantics"}]}
